title: Across the wall
summary: Two long-distance pen friends — one inside a prison, one outside — meet for the first time in the visiting room.

body.speaker: Diem
body.text: It's strange. I have read 187 of your letters, but I have never seen you sit.

body.speaker: Tu
body.text: I thought the same when you walked in. You're shorter than I imagined.

body.speaker: Diem
body.text: Most people are. How are you?

body.speaker: Tu
body.text: Tired, but I always say that. How long do we have?

body.speaker: Diem
body.text: Forty minutes. Then they ring the bell.

body.speaker: Tu
body.text: Then I should ask the important question first. Why did you write to me?

body.speaker: Diem
body.text: You've asked that in letters before. I never answered properly.

body.speaker: Tu
body.text: I know.

body.speaker: Diem
body.text: Three years ago my brother died. He was twenty-eight. I needed to write to someone who could not visit my city, my family, my old apartment. Someone whose life would not bend toward mine if I cried.

body.speaker: Tu
body.text: So you needed distance.

body.speaker: Diem
body.text: I needed a person who could only meet me as words. I thought I would write two letters and stop.

body.speaker: Tu
body.text: And here we are, three years on.

body.speaker: Diem
body.text: Here we are. What about you? Why did you write back?

body.speaker: Tu
body.text: Because nobody else was writing. The chaplain gives us paper. Most prisoners do not use it. I needed to find out if I could still make sentences that mattered to a stranger. Your first letter arrived two weeks after I'd stopped trying.

body.speaker: Diem
body.text: I didn't know that. You never said.

body.speaker: Tu
body.text: I didn't want our letters to be about that.

body.speaker: Diem
body.text: Fair. Tu, I have to ask. Are you angry that I have stayed outside while you have been here?

body.speaker: Tu
body.text: No. Why would I be?

body.speaker: Diem
body.text: I don't know. I would understand if you were.

body.speaker: Tu
body.text: The letters were not a window I was looking through. They were a room I was building. The room was mine. Where you were standing outside it had nothing to do with me.

body.speaker: Diem
body.text: That's beautiful.

body.speaker: Tu
body.text: It's how I survived. Don't romanticise it.

body.speaker: Diem
body.text: I won't. May I ask one more thing?

body.speaker: Tu
body.text: Please.

body.speaker: Diem
body.text: When you are released in eighteen months — will we still write?

body.speaker: Tu
body.text: That is a harder question than it sounds. The letters worked because we could not see each other. We could be precise instead of polite. Outside, we will be in the same world. It changes the form.

body.speaker: Diem
body.text: So no?

body.speaker: Tu
body.text: I didn't say no. I said the form changes. Maybe we will write less and meet more. Maybe one will replace the other. We have time to find out.

body.speaker: Diem
body.text: Forty minutes is short.

body.speaker: Tu
body.text: Yes. But we have practised, in writing, for three years. We are not starting from zero.

annotations.meaningVi: tuyên uý (linh mục/người tâm linh trong trại giam)
được trả tự do
lãng mạn hoá / tô hồng
chính xác, cụ thể (không vòng vo)
hình thức (quan hệ) thay đổi
ba năm sau đó (discourse marker chỉ thời gian đã trôi qua)
tìm hiểu, phát hiện ra (phrasal verb)
nghiêng về phía, bị kéo về phía (ai/điều gì)
có ý nghĩa với một người lạ ('matter to' = quan trọng với ai)
tôi đã thôi cố gắng (past perfect — hành động hoàn tất trước một mốc quá khứ khác)
không liên quan gì đến (fixed expression)

annotations.phrase: a harder question than it sounds
annotations.meaningVi: câu hỏi khó hơn vẻ ngoài của nó (hedging — cảnh báo trước khi trả lời)
thay vì lịch sự (dùng 'instead of' để đối lập hai lựa chọn)
viết thư hồi âm, viết lại (phrasal verb)
trả lời đến nơi đến chốn, trả lời thỏa đáng (collocation)
rung chuông (báo hiệu kết thúc giờ thăm gặp)
bắt đầu từ con số không, bắt đầu lại từ đầu (idiom)
tôi phải hỏi (hedging phrase — dẫn vào câu hỏi nhạy cảm một cách lịch sự)
tôi có thể hỏi không (công thức lịch sự để xin phép đặt câu hỏi)
cái này sẽ thay thế cái kia (cấu trúc 'one … the other' chỉ hai phương án)

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect for accumulated experience
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Cấu trúc 'have + V3' ở B1 thường dùng để nói về điều đã/đang xảy ra trong khoảng thời gian chưa kết thúc, kéo tới hiện tại. Trong bài: 'I have read 187 of your letters' (chưa kết thúc — vẫn đang đọc), 'You have stayed outside while you have been here' (so sánh hai trải nghiệm trải dài). Khi muốn nhấn mạnh tiến trình kéo dài, dùng 'have been + V-ing'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Present perfect ('have + past participle') links past to present. From the dialogue: 'I have read 187 of your letters.' (ongoing) 'You have stayed outside while I have been here.' (two parallel ongoing states). Use the continuous form ('have been + -ing') to emphasise duration.

translationVi:
Diễm: Lạ thật. Em đã đọc 187 lá thư của anh, nhưng chưa từng thấy anh ngồi.
Tú: Anh cũng nghĩ vậy khi em vừa bước vào. Em thấp hơn anh tưởng tượng.
Diễm: Đa số mọi người thế. Anh khoẻ không?
Tú: Mệt, nhưng anh luôn nói thế. Mình có bao lâu?
Diễm: Bốn mươi phút. Rồi họ rung chuông.
Tú: Vậy anh hỏi câu quan trọng trước. Sao em lại viết thư cho anh?
Diễm: Anh từng hỏi trong thư rồi. Em chưa bao giờ trả lời đến nơi đến chốn.
Tú: Anh biết.
Diễm: Ba năm trước anh trai em mất. Anh ấy hai mươi tám tuổi. Em cần viết cho ai đó không thể ghé thành phố em, gia đình em, căn hộ cũ của em. Một người mà cuộc đời họ sẽ không nghiêng về phía em nếu em khóc.
Tú: Vậy em cần khoảng cách.
Diễm: Em cần một người chỉ có thể gặp em bằng chữ. Em đã tưởng em sẽ viết hai lá rồi dừng.
Tú: Vậy mà ba năm sau ta đang ở đây.
Diễm: Ta đang ở đây. Còn anh? Sao anh viết lại?
Tú: Vì không có ai khác viết. Cha tuyên uý phát giấy cho bọn anh. Đa số tù nhân không dùng. Anh cần tìm ra liệu mình còn viết được những câu có nghĩa với một người lạ hay không. Lá thư đầu của em đến sau hai tuần kể từ khi anh thôi cố.
Diễm: Em không hề biết. Anh chưa bao giờ nói.
Tú: Anh không muốn các lá thư của tụi mình nói về chuyện đó.
Diễm: Hợp lý. Tú này, em phải hỏi. Anh có giận vì em ở ngoài còn anh ở đây không?
Tú: Không. Sao anh phải giận?
Diễm: Em không biết. Em sẽ hiểu nếu anh giận.
Tú: Những lá thư không phải là một ô cửa anh nhìn qua. Chúng là một căn phòng anh đang xây. Căn phòng là của anh. Em đứng ở đâu bên ngoài nó chẳng liên quan gì đến anh.
Diễm: Đẹp quá.
Tú: Đó là cách anh sống sót. Đừng tô hồng nó.
Diễm: Em sẽ không. Em hỏi thêm một câu được không?
Tú: Em cứ hỏi.
Diễm: Khi anh được thả ra trong mười tám tháng nữa — chúng ta vẫn sẽ viết chứ?
Tú: Câu đó khó hơn nghe có vẻ. Thư từ hoạt động được vì chúng ta không thể nhìn thấy nhau. Chúng ta có thể chính xác thay vì lịch sự. Ra ngoài, chúng ta sẽ ở cùng một thế giới. Nó đổi hình thức.
Diễm: Vậy là không?
Tú: Anh không nói không. Anh nói hình thức đổi. Có thể chúng ta viết ít hơn và gặp nhiều hơn. Có thể cái này sẽ thay cái kia. Chúng ta có thời gian để tìm ra.
Diễm: Bốn mươi phút thì ngắn.
Tú: Đúng. Nhưng chúng ta đã luyện tập, bằng chữ viết, suốt ba năm. Chúng ta không bắt đầu từ con số không.

questions.prompt: How many of Tu's letters has Diem read?
How much time do they have together?
questions.explanation: 'Forty minutes. Then they ring the bell.'
questions.hint: Diem answers Tu's question 'How long do we have?'

questions.prompt: Why did Diem originally write to Tu?
options: Because she needed someone whose life would not bend toward hers, after her brother died.
Because she was assigned a pen friend at school.
Because she wanted to write a book.
questions.explanation: 'I needed to write to someone who could not visit my city, my family, my old apartment. Someone whose life would not bend toward mine if I cried.'
questions.hint: Look at Diem's longer answer about her brother.

options: Because he needed to find out if he could still write sentences that mattered to a stranger.
questions.explanation: 'I needed to find out if I could still make sentences that mattered to a stranger.'

questions.prompt: What does Tu reveal that he never wrote in their letters?
options: Diem's first letter arrived two weeks after he had stopped trying to write.
He had been moved to a different prison.
He was about to be released.
questions.explanation: 'Your first letter arrived two weeks after I'd stopped trying.'
questions.hint: Tu mentions this as something he 'didn't want our letters to be about.'

questions.prompt: How does Tu describe what the letters were for him?
options: A room he was building, not a window he was looking through.
questions.explanation: 'The letters were not a window I was looking through. They were a room I was building.'
questions.hint: Tu's metaphor in his answer about not being angry.

questions.prompt: Why does Tu tell Diem 'don't romanticise it'?
options: He wants her to see the letters as a survival mechanism, not as something beautiful.
He thinks she is teasing him.
He is angry at her compliment.
He does not want her to write a book about him.
questions.explanation: He follows 'It's how I survived' with 'Don't romanticise it' — survival, not aesthetics.
questions.hint: Look at the line right before he says it.

questions.prompt: What does Diem ask about the future of their writing?
options: Whether they will still write to each other after Tu is released in eighteen months.
Whether they should publish their letters.
Whether Diem can keep writing while travelling.
Whether Tu plans to keep his copies.
questions.explanation: 'When you are released in eighteen months — will we still write?'
questions.hint: Look at Diem's last main question.

questions.prompt: What does Tu mean when he says 'the form changes' after release?
options: Outside prison, they will be able to see each other, so letters may be partly replaced by meeting.
They will have to use a different language.
questions.explanation: 'Maybe we will write less and meet more. Maybe one will replace the other.'
questions.hint: Look at what Tu offers as possibilities.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the dialogue NOT claiming?
options: That all pen-friendships across prison walls will end happily once the prisoner is released.
That distance can make some kinds of honesty possible.
That Diem and Tu have been writing for three years.
That Tu's survival depended on the practice of writing.
questions.explanation: Tu explicitly says the question of whether they'll still write outside is 'harder than it sounds' — not promising a happy outcome.
questions.hint: Re-read Tu's response to 'will we still write?'

cloze.template:
Diem: It's strange. I have read 187 of your {{b1}}, but I have never seen you sit.
Tu: I thought the same when you walked in. You're shorter than I imagined.
Diem: Most people are. How are you?
Tu: Tired, but I always say that. How long do we have?
Diem: {{b2}} minutes. Then they ring the bell.
Tu: Then I should ask the important question first. Why did you write to me?
Diem: You've asked that in letters before. I never answered properly.
Tu: I know.
Diem: Three years ago my brother died. He was twenty-eight. I needed to write to someone who could not visit my city, my family, my old apartment. Someone whose life would not {{b3}} toward mine if I cried.
Tu: So you needed {{b4}}.
Diem: I needed a person who could only meet me as words. I thought I would write two letters and stop.
Tu: And here we are, three years on.
Diem: Here we are. What about you? Why did you write back?
Tu: Because nobody else was writing. The {{b5}} gives us paper. Most prisoners do not use it. I needed to find out if I could still make sentences that mattered to a {{b6}}. Your first letter arrived two weeks after I'd stopped trying.
Diem: I didn't know that. You never said.
Tu: I didn't want our letters to be about that.
Diem: Fair. Tu, I have to ask. Are you {{b7}} that I have stayed outside while you have been here?
Tu: No. Why would I be?
Diem: I don't know. I would understand if you were.
Tu: The letters were not a window I was looking through. They were a {{b8}} I was building. The room was mine. Where you were standing outside it had nothing to do with me.
Diem: That's beautiful.
Tu: It's how I {{b9}}. Don't romanticise it.
Diem: I won't. May I ask one more thing?
Tu: Please.
Diem: When you are released in eighteen months — will we still write?
Tu: That is a harder question than it sounds. The letters worked because we could not see each other. We could be {{b10}} instead of polite. Outside, we will be in the same world. It changes the form.
Diem: So no?
Tu: I didn't say no. I said the form changes. Maybe we will write less and {{b11}} more. Maybe one will replace the other. We have time to find out.
Diem: Forty minutes is short.
Tu: Yes. But we have practised, in writing, for three years. We are not starting from {{b12}}.

blanks.explanation: Diem has read 187 of Tu's letters.
Someone whose life would not bend toward hers.
Tu names the thing she needed: distance.
The chaplain gives the prisoners paper.
He needed to write sentences that mattered to a stranger.
Diem asks if Tu is angry with her.
The letters were a room he was building.
They could be precise instead of polite.
Maybe they will write less and meet more.
They are not starting from zero.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Tu says the letters worked because they could not see each other — they could be 'precise instead of polite.' Is there someone in your life with whom you communicate better in writing than in person? What does the distance let you say that closeness would not?
